title: SessionStart silently removes dev-active marker with no DEV:exit audit line — a session-boundary dev-mode exit leaves the audit trail with an orphaned DEV:enter and no corresponding close
evidence: session-start.py lines 417-422 unconditionally remove the dev-active marker on every SessionStart (via os.remove, silently swallowing OSError). The /ca:arbiter command prose (arbiter.md lines 27-29) acknowledges this: 'If a prior session ended mid-dev (the marker was cleared by SessionStart without an exit line), write the DEV: exit line at the next opportunity.' But the SessionStart hook itself writes nothing to overrides.log when it clears the marker — it only removes the file. The gap: a user enters /ca:dev, the session ends without /ca:arbiter, and the next session's SessionStart removes the marker with no DEV:exit line in the audit log. The overrides.log has a DEV:enter with no corresponding DEV:exit, and no breadcrumb that SessionStart was the implicit exit. The arbiter.md prose asks the LLM to notice this and repair it 'at the next opportunity' — a prose-only instruction to a stateless session with no hook enforcement.
impact: The audit trail (overrides.log) can have unmatched DEV:enter entries that appear as unexplained open dev-mode windows. An auditor reviewing overrides.log cannot tell whether dev mode was active for 5 minutes or 5 days — the exit timestamp is absent. The security-controls.md declares the log append-only and a 'first-class product concern'; this is a completeness gap specific to the dev/arbiter boundary.
recommendation: When SessionStart removes a live dev-active marker, append a synthetic DEV:exit line to overrides.log immediately before removing the marker. Use the same shell-append the command prose specifies ('>>'), emitting the ISO-8601 timestamp and 'BY: session-cleanup' (or the git identity if resolvable). This makes the implicit exit explicit in the audit trail without adding any gate or friction.
acceptance_criteria: When a dev-active marker is found at SessionStart, a DEV:exit line is appended to overrides.log with a timestamp before the marker is removed
The appended line is distinguishable from a manual /ca:arbiter exit (e.g. 'BY: session-cleanup' or a marker field)
A SessionStart on a repo with no dev-active marker does not append anything to overrides.log

title: 'compel a log write' gap confirmed and refined: hook pipeline has no path to force an override/sprint-decision write — a prose-instructed log append can be silently skipped with no detection
evidence: open-questions.md line 19 acknowledges the gap: 'hooks protect logs once written but never force a write.' This finding confirms and refines the scope. The H-05 guards (pre-bash.py 287-293, pre-edit.py 35-49, pre-write.py 29-32) detect and block DESTRUCTIVE operations on overrides.log/triage.log/sprint-log.md — they are integrity guards, not existence guards. No hook fires when the model skips the required append entirely: if /ca:override completes the bypassed action without first appending to overrides.log, no pre-tool or post-tool hook catches the omission. Similarly /ca:sprint's SPRINT.md line 80-83 instructs 'LOG every auto-decision to sprint-log.md' but no hook checks that sprint-log.md was written during a sprint turn. override.md lines 57-60 declare 'MUST write the log line before proceeding — it is not optional' and 'MUST capture an operator identity' — these are prose instructions only. The delta from open-questions.md: the gap applies asymmetrically — sprint auto-decisions and /dev entry/exit have the same structural absence, but /dev entry uniquely has the second gap (observability-001) of a silent SessionStart exit that does not even trigger the prose path.
impact: The core audit-trail guarantee ('every /override, every /sprint auto-decision, every /dev entry/exit MUST be logged') relies entirely on the LLM following prose instructions. A model hallucination, a session interruption, or a future skill edit could skip the append with no enforcement-layer detection. For /ca:sprint autonomous mode specifically, low-confidence auto-decisions that should be logged for morning review could be silently absent from sprint-log.md.
recommendation: The full fix (a post-tool hook that fires on the bypassed action and checks for a fresh log entry) is the 'test-running commit hook' level of invasiveness flagged in open-questions.md. A lightweight mitigation: (1) add a PostToolUse hook on UserPromptSubmit that, when the sprint-log exists and a sprint is active, checks that it was appended to in the last N minutes — a staleness signal rather than a per-decision check; (2) for /override specifically, the overrides.log append precedes the bypassed tool call, so a PreToolUse hook on the bypassed tool checking for a sufficiently recent log entry is feasible and low-invasiveness.
acceptance_criteria: Either: a hook detects that a required audit-log write was skipped and blocks or warns; OR the open question is explicitly promoted to a tracked CONFIRM-NN so it gates a future enforcement decision rather than being prose-only indefinitely
The distinction between the integrity gap (H-05 blocks rewrites) and the completeness gap (nothing forces writes) is documented in security-controls.md §Audit trail

title: farm runTask crash path (Promise rejection handler at line 1596) produces a 'crashed: <message>' note but no run-id or task correlation in the JSONL stream — post-mortem attribution is partial
evidence: Line 1592-1598: when runTask throws (an unhandled rejection from git/fs/etc), the scheduler catches it inline and synthesizes a Result with `status:'escalate', note:'crashed: <e.message>'`. This is appended to farm-results.jsonl (line 1604-1609) and included in farm-report.md/json (writeReport, line 1641-1677). The appended Result includes the task id (line 1596: `{id,...}`), so task-level attribution exists. However: (1) there is no run-level correlation id tying all tasks of a single farm invocation together in the JSONL stream or the report — two concurrent farm runs writing to the same .farm/ directory produce interleaved JSONL lines with no discriminator; (2) the crash note `crashed: ${e?.message ?? e}` omits the stack trace, so a Node.js-internal error (e.g. the spawn TypeError from reliability-004) is logged as a one-line message with no call site. The farm-report.json does have a `ts` field (line 1655) but that is a single run timestamp, not a per-task timestamp or a run-id.
impact: When a farm run crashes mid-flight and an operator reads farm-results.jsonl afterward, they see: task id, status=escalate, note='crashed: ...'. They cannot tell (a) which farm invocation produced the line if two runs are in flight, or (b) where in farm.ts the crash occurred. Diagnosability is limited to the message string; the stack is lost. The reliability lens already flagged the spawn-TypeError crash as reliability-004; this is the observability angle on the same gap.
recommendation: (1) Generate a short run-id at main() startup (e.g. 6 hex chars from crypto.randomBytes) and include it in every JSONL line and in the farm-report.json header — standard JSONL correlation. (2) In the crash handler (line 1595-1597), include err.stack (truncated) in the note field so a post-mortem can identify the call site. Both are low-effort and improve operator diagnosability with no behavioral change.
acceptance_criteria: Each line in farm-results.jsonl carries a run_id field that matches the farm-report.json run_id for the same invocation
The crash note in the escalate result includes the error stack (truncated to a reasonable length) when available
Two concurrent farm runs writing to the same directory produce distinguishable JSONL lines

title: defaultCloneRepo in ca-sandbox swallows clone failure silently at the spawnAsync layer — a failed clone returns exit code 1 but no stderr is captured or surfaced
evidence: spawnAsync (lines 146-152) is: spawn('docker', args, {env, stdio:'ignore'}); it resolves with the exit code but never captures stdout or stderr (stdio:'ignore' discards all streams). defaultCloneRepo (lines 161-163) calls spawnAsync and returns the exit code. createSandbox (lines 307-309) checks `if (cloneCode !== 0) throw new Error('ca-sandbox: clone of ${url} into ${volumeName} failed (exit ${cloneCode})')` — the error message contains only the url and the exit code. If docker run or git clone fail (network error, auth failure, disk full, invalid url that passed validateRepoUrl), the error message an operator sees is 'clone of https://... failed (exit 1)' with no git stderr showing what went wrong. The reliability lens (reliability-002) already flagged the unchecked docker create exit; this is the parallel observability gap on the clone step.
impact: A failed clone during sandbox creation gives the operator only the exit code. Common failure modes (network connectivity, git credential issues, disk full inside the docker volume) produce identical exit-1 messages that are unactionable without re-running manually. The operator cannot tell from the error whether to retry, fix credentials, or free disk space.
recommendation: Change spawnAsync to capture stderr (use stdio:['ignore','ignore','pipe'] or pipe all streams and capture stderr only), and thread the captured stderr into the error message thrown in createSandbox. Alternatively, replace spawnAsync with the existing spawnSync pattern used elsewhere in create.ts and capture r.stderr. A bounded slice (last 500 bytes) of git's stderr is sufficient for diagnosis without flooding the caller.
acceptance_criteria: A failed clone surfaces actionable git stderr in the thrown error (e.g. 'fatal: repository not found' or 'error: RPC failed')
The clone error message distinguishes at minimum between a docker-level failure and a git-level failure
The fix does not change the behavior on success

title: H-05 log-guard regex does not fire on fd-redirect and process-substitution spellings of truncation — known gap is undocumented in security-controls.md §Audit trail
evidence: pre-bash.py comment at lines 63-67 explicitly documents the gap: 'Known limitations — this regex catches the common `> file` and `>| file` (force-clobber) truncation forms but NOT every shell spelling that produces a new file descriptor on the log. Specific gaps: triple-chevron (`>>>`), and file-descriptor forms like `exec 3>.codearbiter/overrides.log`.' The regex LOG_TRUNC_RE (line 73) uses a lookbehind that excludes `>>` (append) and admits `>` and `>|`; it cannot see `exec N>file` because that form lacks an adjacent filename token the regex can anchor to. security-controls.md §Audit trail (lines 148-155) states 'The pre-bash.py H-05 guard and the pre-write.py / pre-edit.py H-05 guards enforce this at every tool-call boundary' — no mention of the regex scope or the known gaps. A consumer reading security-controls.md gets a stronger guarantee than what is actually enforced.
impact: security-controls.md overstates the enforcement coverage. An operator auditing the system against security-controls.md would conclude the audit logs are fully protected at every tool-call boundary, but fd-redirect and process-substitution spellings are not caught by the Bash hook. This is a documentation gap, not a new behavioral gap — the code comment is accurate, but the security-controls summary is not. Operator trust in the guarantee is misfounded for those edge cases.
recommendation: Add a parenthetical to security-controls.md §Audit trail noting the known regex scope (catches common truncation forms; `exec N>`, `>>>`, and process-substitution spellings are out of scope — accepted residual risk per the inline code comment). This aligns the policy document with the actual enforcement boundary without requiring a code change. The accepted-residual-risk designation is already present in the code; it should be mirrored in the policy doc.
acceptance_criteria: security-controls.md §Audit trail accurately describes the H-05 guard's scope, including the known gaps
The existing code comment in pre-bash.py remains the authoritative technical detail; security-controls.md references or summarizes it
